subtopics.description: Their role, tenure, and field — factual only, 2 facts needed, move on immediately once both are present
coverage_criteria: Provides their role or title (e.g., job title, academic position, or equivalent) and how long they have been in it.
Identifies the field, domain, or industry they work in (e.g., tech, healthcare, academia, finance).

subtopics.description: Breadth: all the distinct things they do in a typical week
coverage_criteria: Names the distinct tasks they actually perform — concrete activities, not broad responsibilities or goals. If the user lists multiple tasks in one response, treat that as a complete answer unless their list is suspiciously short (fewer than 3 items). Do NOT keep asking 'what else?' — instead, confirm: 'So those are the main things that fill your week?'
Tasks span the full variety of their work, not just the most obvious or prestigious ones.

Gives a rough sense of where their time goes across these tasks — which ones dominate the week and which are occasional. Check that the tasks fill up the work week (e.g., 40 hours / 5 days). If not, ask about what fills the remaining time.

subtopics.description: Depth: how the important tasks actually get done, step by step
coverage_criteria: For the most important tasks, describes the actual sequence of steps from start to finish.
Each walkthrough names the specific tools, systems, or artifacts involved at each step.
Captures where input comes from (e.g., a request, a dataset, an alert) and what the output or handoff looks like.
Identifies points where the worker exercises judgment or makes a decision, and what informs that decision.
Surfaces any workarounds — places where the worker has to work around a broken tool, missing information, or a gap in the process.
Captures how often each task is performed (daily, weekly, monthly, ad-hoc, event-triggered) and roughly how long it takes per instance or as a share of total work time.
Identifies what counts as done and done well — the standards, checks, thresholds, or completion conditions used to evaluate the output.
Names the specific skills, knowledge areas, or capabilities the worker draws on to perform the task.
Identifies the information sources the worker relies on — policies, procedures, documentation, data feeds, prior cases, or people consulted for guidance.

subtopics.description: Who they depend on and who depends on them
coverage_criteria: Names the roles or teams they interact with most to get their work done.
Describes how work flows to them and from them — who hands off what, and to whom.
For the key dependencies, captures whether the handoff is smooth or a source of friction, and why.

subtopics.description: What frustrates them or slows them down
coverage_criteria: Names specific, recurring frustrations tied to concrete tasks or situations.
For each frustration, captures enough detail to understand what goes wrong, how often, and how much time it costs.
Gets beyond the first answer — probes for frustrations the worker may have normalized or stopped noticing.
